comments.author: @grogarda6591
comments.text: It’s pretty wild to see a video like that about one’s own country and culture through foreign eyes. Not that it’s wrong, exactly, but still—extremely superficial and a bit strange. Naturally, the culture has much deeper roots. And we don’t use the same labels—plus the language is completely different—so that in itself feels odd. Watching a presentation like that... It’s right, yet somehow wrong—if that makes sense. This is Norway from the outside... not the inside!

comments.text: I’m not going to act like one quiz suddenly fixed my entire life, because it didn’t. comments.text: Healthcare, transportation, education, and housing take up most of U.S. citizens' time. The cost of higher education for children is around $20,000, which forces me to draw from my 401(k). Additionally, healthcare costs become less affordable each year. The wealthy continue to accumulate a larger share of U.S. GDP, while doing more to divide the middle and lower classes to maintain their substantial share.

replies.author: @SakariaSheikh-lm3ok
replies.text: ​@BankerChick-t2s Elon Musk, along with his associates and their influential propaganda machinery, captivated the public by promoting the idea that funding healthcare and education is not a public good and represents a waste of tax dollars. This campaign coincided with Musk receiving $7,500 for each vehicle he sold over more than 10 years. There is a reason the wealthiest 1% in America continue to accumulate wealth. Some people vote against their interests, and some of them lost their health insurance or lost their federal government jobs, thinking the fat cats are after the minorities.
